central_question.question: Does deferring EpochManager wiring into Snapshot/CommitVersion violate this subtask's literal acceptance criteria ('each snapshot increments its epoch's refcount on creation')?
central_question.judgment: NOT a genuine gap. The original planner decomposition (.cdr/runs/2026-07-03/002-planner/plan.json, written before implementation) explicitly scopes 2a.2.1's impacted modules to engine/mvcc/gc.go and gc_test.go ONLY, excluding read.go/write.go, and separately assigns 'a version file is deleted only once its epoch's refcount is zero' to 2a.2.2, whose AC/test requires 'hold some snapshots open, run compactor' -- i.e. actual Snapshot<->EpochManager wiring is pre-planned to land alongside the compactor in 2a.2.2, not 2a.2.1. The 2a.2.1 test spec's phrase 'open/close overlapping snapshots' is satisfied by directly calling AcquireCurrentEpoch/Release on EpochManager in gc_test.go, modeling snapshot lifecycle abstractly -- consistent with what was built. This is pre-existing scope from planning, not the implementer retroactively narrowing the requirement. Accepted as a reasonably-scoped incremental step.
central_question.residual_risk: Until 2a.2.2 actually wires NewSnapshot->AcquireCurrentEpoch and adds Snapshot.Close()->Release, the literal English of the AC ('each snapshot increments...') is unmet by the real Snapshot type -- EpochManager today has zero live callers anywhere in the codebase. This is flagged as PASS_WITH_COMMENTS rather than plain PASS specifically so 2a.2.2's verification explicitly confirms the wiring actually lands as promised; if 2a.2.2 ships without wiring Snapshot end-to-end, this should be escalated to CHANGES_REQUESTED at that point.

requirements.evidence: Refcounting primitive (increment on acquire, decrement on release, never negative, reaches 0 on full release) is implemented and tested exactly per AC text when read as 'the refcounting primitive itself'. See central_question for the literal-vs-scoped nuance.

architecture_conformance.evidence: Matches docs/LLD/mvcc.md's Garbage collection section verbatim ('each snapshot increments an epoch's refcount on start and decrements on completion; a version is eligible for GC once its epoch's refcount reaches zero and it is not the current version'). Global (not per-fileID) counter choice is documented and reasoned in gc.go doc comments and plan.md; consistent with LLD's 'simplified Postgres-vacuum-style visibility scheme' framing. No modification to read.go/write.go, matching planner's own module scoping for this subtask.

regression_risk.evidence: gc.go is 100% new/additive; no existing symbol modified. Full suite go test ./engine/... ./api/... -race -count=1 passes (engine, engine/btree, engine/catalog, engine/mvcc, engine/wal all ok; graph/loadtest/rpc/split/api have no test files). engine/mvcc specifically: 27.5s, all PASS including pre-existing TestConcurrentReadersWriters, TestSnapshotRead, TestVersionWriter, TestCurrentVersionCAS, TestVersionCASWAL.

edge_cases.evidence: Traced by hand: (1) non-LIFO release order (test releases 3 acquisitions of epoch 1 in arbitrary order) -- correct, since a bare per-epoch counter doesn't care about acquisition order. (2) double-release on an already-zero epoch: Release checks `count, ok := refcounts[epoch]; if !ok || count <= 0 { return error }` BEFORE decrementing, so refcount is structurally guaranteed to never go negative; test explicitly asserts a non-nil error (not panic) and RefCount stays 0 afterward -- genuine, not a fake assertion. (3) Release on an epoch number never acquired at all (epoch 999): also correctly errors via the same `!ok` check. (4) MinReferencedEpoch with a gap: hand-traced scenario 'epochs 1,2,3 with live refs on 1 and 3, none on 2' -> MinReferencedEpoch()=1 (the true global minimum among refcount>0 entries), which is the CORRECT and safe answer for GC: a live snapshot at epoch 1 could still be reading a version that predates epoch 1, so GC must not reclaim anything superseded before epoch 1 regardless of epoch 2 being empty. This is conservative (foregoes reclaiming epoch-2-only garbage) by design, matching the LLD's 'simplified' global watermark scheme, not a correctness bug.

security.evidence: No new external inputs, no I/O, no injection surface. Pure in-memory counter/map guarded by a single mutex.

performance.evidence: O(1) Acquire/Release/CurrentEpoch/AdvanceEpoch under a single mutex; MinReferencedEpoch is O(#distinct-nonzero-epochs) map scan, acceptable for a GC-frequency operation. No obvious hot-path impact since nothing calls this yet.

maintainability.evidence: Doc comments are unusually thorough and explicitly cross-reference the LLD and future subtasks. Minor nit: AcquireCurrentEpoch's name emphasizes 'get the current epoch' but its primary side effect is incrementing a refcount (like a reference-counted acquire); a future caller skimming the name alone could plausibly assume it's a pure read like CurrentEpoch(). The doc comment does clarify this well, so this is a naming style nit, not a defect.

test_coverage.evidence: TestEpochRefcount covers: initial state, sequential acquire with per-step assertions, cross-epoch AdvanceEpoch while old epoch still referenced, non-LIFO release with per-step assertions, MinReferencedEpoch at multiple transition points, double-release and never-acquired-epoch error paths. TestEpochRefcountConcurrent runs 20 goroutines x 50 ops with -race, asserting terminal invariants (all refcounts 0, MinReferencedEpoch ok=false). Ran TestEpochRefcount and TestEpochRefcountConcurrent at -count=10 -race: no flakes.

confidence.evidence: Verified build/vet, full race test suite, hand-traced edge cases, and cross-checked the central scoping question against the pre-implementation planner artifact (plan.json from 2026-07-03), which independently corroborates the implementer's scoping decision was pre-planned, not invented post-hoc.

possible_improvements: Consider renaming AcquireCurrentEpoch to something like AcquireEpoch or IncrefCurrentEpoch to make the refcount side effect more discoverable from the name alone.

2a.2.2's verification MUST explicitly confirm NewSnapshot calls AcquireCurrentEpoch and a Snapshot.Close() (new method) calls Release -- this is the single most important carry-forward check from this subtask.

MinReferencedEpoch's global (not per-fileID) watermark is intentionally coarse; document this trade-off (versions between two live-referenced epochs cannot be reclaimed even though nothing references the epoch in between) directly in the GC/compactor design doc for 2a.2.2 so it isn't mistaken for a bug later.

commands_run: (cd engine && go build ./... && go vet ./...)  -> OK
go test ./engine/mvcc/... -race -run TestEpochRefcount$ -count=10  -> OK, no flakes
go test ./engine/mvcc/... -race -run TestEpochRefcountConcurrent$ -count=10  -> OK, no flakes
go test ./engine/... ./api/... -race -count=1  -> all PASS, zero regressions
